customer_addr:
18609 SE State Route Y
DEARBORN, MO 64439

citations.narrative:
* Approximately 40% of the floor was covered with bedding, feathers, vermin waste, brown dust, webs, hair and pieces or
white Styrofoam insulation. Under the sink there was a pile of loose pieces of white Styrofoam. The pieces of insulation
and vermin waste indicate an ongoing vermin infestation. The ceiling and a grey plastic stand had black to reddish brown
grimy spots covering approximately 30% of their surfaces. This indicates there are not enough provisions to remove or
dispose of animal and food waste, bedding, grime and insect waste on hard surfaces and the debris. As a result, there is
a vermin infestation, and numerous insects flying around in the building. This can increase the potential for other disease
hazards for the 37 guinea pigs.
For the prevention and accumulation of animal and food waste, bedding, black to brown grimy spots on the hard surfaces,
debris, and to minimize vermin, insects and disease hazards for all guinea pigs, additional provisions of removal and
disposal must be implemented.
To be correct by, 13-April-2026.

* There was a live visible mouse under the sink area during the inspection that quickly scampered into hiding. The
licensee was asked about the free roaming mouse, and stated that the wild mice had made an opening through the
floor/wall of the building. This opening of the building has not been closed and or repaired yet. This gave the wild mice
free access inside the building. There were numerous mouse droppings on the table where the clean feeding bowls were
stored. The mouse fecal waste droppings were on the floor around the sink area and husbandry work area on top of a
cardboard box. Numerous pieces of Styrofoam insulation from inside the wall were on the floor around the sink area.
Similarly, there was a pile of loose Styrofoam insulation under the sink.
Numerous small insects (small flies) were observed flying around the ceiling in the center part of the building. The
licensee had two separate rolls sticky-tape for flies at the opposite ends of the building. However, only a small portion of
the sticky-tape roll was exposed to catch small flies and or insects.
The presence of mice and small flies indicates that there is not an established and maintained program to control insects
and mammalian pests. As a result, this can increase the disease hazards for the 37 guinea pigs.
For the prevention of disease hazards for all guinea pigs, an effective program to control insects and mammalian pests
must be established and maintained.
To be corrected by, 13-April-2026.
